It was for that reason I wanted to ask one or two supplementary questions.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Siemers, if there were any allegations that in any way bore on the case against Defendant Raeder, the Tribunal would of course allow you to ask questions; but there is no allegation which in any way connects the Defendant Raeder with the allegations about the Katyn woods.

turns.speaker: DR. SIEMERS
turns.text: I am grateful to the Tribunal for that statement, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Mr. President, may I be allowed to ask something else? May I have the question put to the Prosecution, who is to be made responsible for the Katyn case?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I do not propose to answer questions of that sort. The Prosecution may now cross-examine if they want to.

turns.speaker: CHIEF COUNSELLOR OF JUSTICE L. N. SMIRNOV (Assistant Prosecutor for the U.S.S.R.)
turns.text: Please tell me, Witness, since when, exactly, have you been in the Smolensk district territory?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I have already answered that question: since the second half of November 1941.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please answer me further, where were you prior to the second part of 1941? Did you in any way have anything to do with Katyn or Smolensk or this district in general? Were you there personally in September and October 1941?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, I was not there.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: That is to say that you were not there, either in September or in October 1941, and therefore do not know what happened at that time in the Katyn forest?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I was not there at that time, but I mentioned earlier on that...

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: No, I am actually only interested in a short question. Were you there personally or not? Were you able to see for yourself what was happening there or not?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: He says he was not there.

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, I was not there.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: He said he was not there in September or October 1941.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. President. [Turning to the witness.] Maybe you recall the family names of the Russian women workers who were employed at the country house in the woods?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Those female workers were not working in different houses. They merely worked as auxiliary kitchen personnel in our Dnieper Castle. I have not known their names at all.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: That means that the Russian women workers were employed only in the villa situated in Katyn forest where the staff headquarters were located?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I believe that question was not translated well. I did not understand it.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I asked you whether the Russian women workers were employed exclusively in the villa in Kosig Gory where the staff headquarters were located? Is that right?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: The women workers worked for the regimental headquarters as kitchen help, and as kitchen helpers they worked on our premises; and by our premises I mean this particular house with the adjoining houses-for instance, the stables, the garage, the cellars, the boiler room.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I will mention a few names of German military employees. Will you please tell me whether they belonged to your unit? First Lieutenant Rex?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: First Lieutenant Rex was my regimental adjutant.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please tell me, was he already assigned to that unit before your arrival at Katyn?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Yes, he was there before I came.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: He was your adjutant, was he not?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Yes, he was my adjutant.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Lieutenant Hodt? Hodt or Hoth?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Lieutenant Hodt is right; but what question are you putting about Lieutenant Hodt?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I am only questioning you about whether he belonged to your unit or not.

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Lieutenant Hodt was a member of the regiment. Whether . . .

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, that is what I was asking. He belonged to the regiment which you commanded, to your army unit?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I did not say by that that he was a member of the regimental staff,, but that he belonged to the regiment. The regiment consisted of three units.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: But he lived in the same villa, did he not?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: That I do not know. When I arrived he was not there. I ordered him to report to me there for the first time.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I will enumerate a few other names. Corporal Rose, Private Giesecken, Oberfeldwebel Krimmenski, Feldwebel Lummert, a cook named Gustav. Were these members of the Armed Forces who were billeted in the villa?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: May I ask you to mention the names individually once again, and I will answer you individually.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Feldwebel Lummert?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Corporal Rose?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And I believe, if my memory serves me correctly, Storekeeper Giesecke.

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: That man's name was Giesecken.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, that is right. I did not pronounce this name quite correctly. These were all your people or at least they belonged to your unit, did they not?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And you assert that you did not know what these people were doing in September and October 1941?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: As I was not there, I cannot tell you for certain.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: We will adjourn now. [A recess was taken.]

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: May I continue? Mr. President, since the witness has stated that he cannot give any testimony concerning the period of September to October 1941, I will limit myself to very short questions. [Turning to the witness.] Witness, would you please point out the location of the villa and the forest with respect to the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway? Did the estate cover a large area?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: My sketch is on a scale of 1 to 100,000 and is drawn from memory. I estimate, therefore, that the graves were situated 200 to 300 meters directly west of the road to our Dnieper Castle, and 200 to 300 meters south of the Smolensk-Vitebsk road so that the Dnieper Castle lay a further 600 meters away.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat that?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: South of the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway, approximately 15 kilometers west of Smolensk. According to the scale 1 to 100,000, as far as one is able to draw such a sketch accurately from memory, the site of these graves was 200 to 300 meters to the south, and a further 600 meters to the south, directly on the northern bend of the Dnieper, was situated our regimental staff quarters, the Dnieper Castle.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Consequently, the villa was approximately 600 meters away from the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, that is not correct. What I said...

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please give a more or less exact figure. What was the distance between the highway and the villa, please?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I just mentioned it in my testimony, that is to say, the graves were about 200 to 300 meters away, and there were a further 600 meters to the castle, therefore, in all about 900 to 1,000 meters. It might have been 800 meters, but that is the approximate distance as can also be seen by this sketch.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I am not following this. Your question, Colonel Smirnov, was: How far was it from the road to what you called the country house? Was it not?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: No, Mr. President, I asked how far was the villa from the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What do you mean by the "Villa"?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: The headquarters of the unit commanded by the witness in 1941 was quartered in a villa, and this villa was situated not far from the Dnieper River, at a distance of about 900 meters from the highroad. The graves were nearer to the highway. I would like to know how far away were the headquarters from the highway, and how far away from the highway were the graves in Katyn forest.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What you want to know is: Mow far was the house in which the headquarters was situated from the highway? Is that right?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, that is exactly what I wanted to know, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: You put two questions to me: first of all, how far were the graves from the highway; and secondly, how far was the house from the highway. I will repeat the answer once more, the house was 800 to 1,000 meters south of the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: One minute, please. I asked you primarily only about the house. Your answer concerning the graves was given on your own initiative. Now I will ask you about the graves, how far were these mass graves from the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: From 200 to 300 meters. It might also have been 350 meters.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Consequently, the graves were 200 or 300 meters from the main road which connected two important centers? Is that right?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Yes, indeed. They were at a distance of 200 to 300 meters south of this, and I may say that at my time this was the most frequented road I ever saw in Russia.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: That was just what I was asking you. Now, please tell me: Was the Katyn wood a real forest, or was it, rather, a park or a grove?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Up to now I have only spoken about the wood of Katyn. This wood of Katyn is the fenced-in wooded area of about 1 square kilometer; which I drew in my sketch. This wood is of mixed growth, of older and younger trees. There were many birch trees in this little wood. However, there were clearings in this wood, and I should say that from 30 to 40 percent was cleared. One could see this from the stumps of newly felled trees. Under no circumstances could you describe this wood as a park; at any rate one could not come to such a conclusion. Fighting had' taken place in this wood, as one could still see trenches and fox holes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, but anyway, you would not call Katyn wood a real forest since it was relatively a small grove in the immediate vicinity of the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway. Is that right?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, that is not right. It was a forest. The entire Katyn forest was a regular forest which began near our grove and extended far beyond that. Of this Katyn forest, which was a mixed forest, part of it had been fenced in, and this part, extending over 1 square kilometer, was what we called the little Katyn wood, but it did belong to this entire wooded region south of the highway. The forest began with our little wood and extended to the west.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I am not interested in the general characteristics of the wood. I would like you to answer the following short question: Were the mass graves located in this grove?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: The mass graves were situated directly west of our entrance drive in a clearing in the wood, where there was a growth of young trees.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, but this clearing, this growth of young trees, was located inside this small grove, near the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway, is that correct?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: It was 200 to 300 meters south of the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway, and directly west of the entrance drive leading from this road to the Dnieper Castle. I have marked this spot on my sketch with a fairly large white dot.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: One more question. As far as you know did the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway exist before the German occupation of Smolensk, or was it constructed only after the occupation?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: When I arrived in Russia at the end of November 1941, everything was covered with snow. Later I got the impression that this was an old road, whereas the road Minsk-Moscow was newer. That was my impression.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I understand. Now tell me, under what circumstances, or rather, when did you first discover the cross in the grove?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I cannot tell the exact date. My soldiers told me about it, and on one occasion when I was going past there, about the beginning of January 1942-it could also have been at the end of December 1941-I saw. this cross rising above the snow.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: This means you saw it already in 1941 or at the latest the beginning of 1942?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: That is what I have just testified.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, certainly. Now, please be more specific concerning the date when a wolf brought you to this cross Was it in winter or summer and what year?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: It was the beginning of 1943.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: In 1943? And around the cross you saw bones, did you not?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: No?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, at first I did not see them. In order to find out whether I had not been mistaken about seeing a wolf, for it seemed rather impossible that a wolf should be so near to Smolensk, I examined the tracks together with a gamekeeper and found traces of scratching on the ground. However, the ground was frozen hard, there was snow on the ground and I did not see anything further there. Only later on, after it had been thawing my men found various bones. However, this was months later and then, at a suitable opportunity I showed these bones to a doctor and he said that these were human bones. Thereupon I said, "Then most likely it is a grave, left as a result of the fighting which has taken place here," and that the war graves registration officer would have to take care of the graves in the same way in which we were taking care of other graves of fallen soldiers. That was the reason why I spoke to this gentleman-but only after the snow had melted.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: By the way, did you personally see the Katyn graves?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Open or before they were opened?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Open, yes.

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: When they were open I had constantly to drive past these graves, as generally they were approximately 30 meters away from the entrance drive. Therefore, I could hardly go past without taking any notice of them.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I am interested in the following: Do you remember what the depth of the layer of earth was, which covered the mass of human bodies in these graves?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: That I do not know. I have already said that I was so nauseated by the stench which we had to put up with for several weeks, that when I drove past I closed the windows of my car and rushed through as fast as I could.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: However, even if you only casually glanced at those graves, perhaps you noticed whether the layer of earth covering the corpses was deep or shallow? Was it several centimeters or several meters deep? Maybe Professor Butz told you something about it?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: As commander of a signal regiment I was concerned with a region which was almost half as large as Greater Germany and I was on the road a great deal. My work was not entirely carried out at the regimental battle headquarters. Therefore, in general, from Monday or Tuesday until Saturday I was with my units. For that reason, when I drove through, I did cast an occasional glance at these graves; but I was not especially interested in the details and I did not speak to Professor Butzabout such details. For this reason I have only a faint recollection of this matter.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: According to the material submitted to the High Tribunal by the Soviet Prosecution, it has been established that the bodies were buried at a depth of l 1/2 to 2 meters. I wonder where you met a wolf who could scratch the ground up to a depth of 2 meters.

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I did not meet this wolf, but I saw it.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Tell me please, why you started the exhumation on these mass graves in March 1943 only, after having discovered the cross and learned about the mass graves already in 1941?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: That was not my concern, but a matter for the army group. I have already told you that in the course of 1942 the stories became more substantial. I frequently heard about them and spoke about it to Colonel Von Gersdorff, Chief of Intelligence, Army Group Center, who intimated to me that he knew all about this matter and with that my obligation ended. I had reported what I had seen and heard. Apart from that, all this matter did not concern me and I did not concern myself with it. I had enough worries of my own.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And now the last question. Please tell me who were these two persons with whom you had this conversation, and maybe you can recollect the names of the couple who told you about the shootings in the Katyn woods?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: This couple lived in a small house about 800 to 1,000 meters north of the entrance to our drive leading to the Vitebsk road. I do not recall their names.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: So you do not remember the names of this couple?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, I do not recall the names.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: So you heard about the Katyn events from a couple whose names you do not remember, and you did not hear anything about it from other local inhabitants?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Please repeat the question for me.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Consequently, you heard about these Katyn events only from this couple, whose names you do not remember? From none of the other local inhabitants did you hear anything about the events in Katyn?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I personally heard the facts only from this couple, whereas my soldiers told me the stories current among the other inhabitants.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Do you know that during the investigation of the Katyn affair, or rather of the Katyn provocation, posters were placarded by the German Police in the streets of Smolensk, promising a reward to anyone giving any information in connection with the Katyn event? It was signed by Lieutenant Voss.

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I personally did not see that poster. Lieutenant Voss is known to me by name only.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And the very last question. Do you know of the report of the Extraordinary State Commission concerning Katyn?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Do you mean the Russian White Paper when you mention this report?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: No, I mean the report of the Soviet Extraordinary State Commission, concerning Katyn, the Soviet report. - AHRENS: Yes, I read that report.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Therefore, you are acquainted with the fact that the Extraordinary State Commission names you as being one of the persons responsible for the crimes committed in Katyn?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: It mentions a Lieutenant Colonel Ames.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I have no further questions, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Stahmer, do you wish to re-examine?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Witness, just a little while ago you said that you did not know when First Lieutenant Hodt joined your staff. Do you know when he joined the regiment?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I know that he belonged to the regiment during the Russian campaign and actually right from the beginning.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: That is, he belonged to the regiment from the beginning?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Yes. He belonged to this regiment ever since the beginning of the Russian campaign.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Just one more question dealing with your discussion with Professor Butz. Did Professor Butz mention anything about the last dates on the letters which he found?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Me told me about the spring of 1940. He also showed me this diary and I looked at it and I also saw the dates, but I do not recall in detail just which date or dates they were. But they ended with the spring of 1940.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Therefore no documents were found of a later date?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: Professor Butz told me that no documents or notes were found which might have given indications of a later date, and he expressed his conviction that these shootings must have taken place in the spring of 1940.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Mr. President, I have no further questions to put to the witness.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Witness, can you not remember exactly when Professor Butz discussed with you the date at which the corpses were buried in the mass graves?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: May I ask to have the question repeated?

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: When did Professor Butz speak to you about the mass graves and assert that the burial of the corpses must have taken place in the spring of 1940?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I cannot tell you the date exactly, but it was in the spring of 1943, before these exhumations had started-I beg your pardon-he told me that he had been instructed to undertake the exhumation and during the exhumations he was with me from time to time; therefore it may have been in May or the end of April. In the middle of May he gave me details of his exhumations and told me among other things that which I have testified here. I cannot now tell you exactly on which days Professor Butz visited me.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: So far as I can remember, you stated that Professor Butz arrived in Katyn. When did he actually arrive there?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: In the spring of 1940 Professor Butz came to me and told me that on instructions of the army group, he was to undertake exhumations in my woods. The exhumations were started, and in the course of...

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: You say 1940? Or perhaps the translation is wrong?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: 1943, in the spring of 1943. A few weeks after the beginning of the exhumations, Professor Butz visited me, when I happened to be there, and informed me; or, rather, he discussed this matter with me, and he told me that to which I have testified here. It may have been the middle of May 1943.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: According to your testimony, I understood you to say in answer to a question put by the defense counsel, that Professor Butz asserted that the shootings had taken place in the spring of 1940 before the arrival of the commission for the exhumations. Is that correct?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: May I repeat once more that Professor Butz...

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: It is not necessary to repeat what you have already said. I am only asking you, is it correct or not? Maybe the translation was incorrect, or maybe your testimony was incorrect at the beginning.

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: I did not understand the question just put to me. That is the reason why I wanted to explain this once more. I do not know just what is meant by this last question. May I ask this question be repeated?

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: At the beginning, when you were interrogated by the defense counsel, I understood you to say that Professor Butz told you that the shooting had taken place in the spring of 1940, that is before the arrival of the commission for the exhumations.

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, that has not been understood correctly. I testified that Professor Butz came to me and told me that he was to make exhumations since it concerned my woods. These exhumations then took place, and approximately 6 to 8 weeks later Professor Butz came to me-of course, he visited me on other occasions as well-but approximately 6 to 8 weeks later he came to me and told me that he was convinced that, as a result of his discoveries, he was now able to fix the date of the shootings. This statement which he made to me, refers approximately to the middle of May.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Were you present when the diary and the other documents which were shown to you by Professor Butz were found?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: You do not know where he found the diary and other documents?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, that I do not know.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: When did you first report to superior authority the fact that you suspected that there was a grave there?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: At first, I was not suspicious. I have already mentioned that fighting had taken place there; and at first I did not attach any importance to the stories told to me and did not give this matter any credence. I believed that it was a question of soldiers who had been killed there of war graves, like several in the vicinity.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You are not answering my question. I am asking you, when did you first report to superior authority that there was a grave there?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: In the course of the summer 1942 I spoke to Colonel Von Gersdorff about these stories which had come to my knowledge. Gersdorff told me that he had heard that too, and that ended my conversation with Von Gersdorff. He did not believe it to be true; in any case he was not thoroughly convinced. That I do not know, however. Then in the spring of 1943, when the snow had melted, the bones which had been found there were brought to me, and I then telephoned to the officer in charge of war graves and told him that apparently there were some soldiers' graves here. That was before Professor Butz had visited me.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Did you make any report in writing?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, I did not do that.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Never?

turns.speaker: AHRENS
turns.text: No, I was not in any way concerned with this matter.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The witness can retire.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Then, as another witness, I should like to call Lieutenant Reinhard von Eichborn.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes. [The witness Von Eichborn took the stand.] Will you state your full name please.

turns.speaker: REINHARD VON EICHBORN (witness)
turns.text: Reinhard von Eichborn. THE PRESIDENT: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear by God-the Almighty and Omniscient-that I win speak the pure truth-and will withhold and add nothing. [The witness repeated the oath.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You may sit down.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Witness, what is your occupation?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Assistant judge.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were you called up for service in the German Armed Forces during this war?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Yes, in August 1939.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And what was your unit?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Army Group Signal Regiment 537.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And what was your rank?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: At the outbreak of the war, platoon leader and lieutenant.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And at the end?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: First lieutenant.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were you on the Eastern Front during the war?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Yes, from the beginning.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: With your regiment?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: No, from 1940 onward, on the staff of Army Group Center.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Apart from this Regiment 537, was there an Engineer Battalion 537?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: In the sphere of the Army Group Center there was no Engineer Battalion 537.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: When did you arrive with your unit in the vicinity of Katyn?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: About 20 September the staff of Army Group Center transferred its headquarters to Smolensk, that is to say in the Smolensk region.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Where had you been stationed before?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: How am I to understand this question?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Where did you come from?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: We came from Borisov.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: One moment. The witness said 20 September. That does not identify the year.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: In what year was this 20 September?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: 20 September 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Was Regiment 537 already there at that time?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: The staff of Regiment 537 was transferred at about the same time together with the staff of the army group to the place where the headquarters of the army group was. Advance units had already been stationed there previously, in order to set up communication facilities.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And where was this staff accommodated?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: The staff of Army Group Signal Regiment 537 was accommodated in the so-called Dnieper Castle.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Where was the advance unit?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: The advance unit may have occupied this building, too-or at least a part of this advance unit did-to safeguard this building for the regimental staff. l July 46

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know who was in command of this advance unit?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Lieutenant Hodt was in command of this advance unit.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: When did this advance unit come to Katyn?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Smolensk fell on about 17 July 1941. The army group had planned to put up its headquarters in the immediate vicinity of Smolensk, and, after this group had selected its quarters, this region was seized immediately after the fall of the city. The advance unit arrived at the same time as this area was seized, and that was probably in the second half of July of 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Therefore the advance unit was there from July of 1941 until 20 September 1941?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And the entire staff was there from 20 September 1941?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Yes. It may be that part of the staff arrived somewhat later, but the majority of the staff arrived on 20 September.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Are you speaking of the staff of the army group or the staff of the signal regiment?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: I am speaking of both staffs, because the moving of large staffs such as that of an army group could not be undertaken in 1 day; usually 2 to 3 days were needed for that. The operations of the signal corps had to be assured, and therefore the regiment had to leave some of the staff behind until the entire staff had been moved.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Where was the advance unit accommodated?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: At least part of the advance unit was accommodated in the Dnieper Castle. Some of the others were in the neighborhood of those places where later on the companies were billeted. The reason for that was to keep the billets ready for this regiment until the bulk of it had been moved.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How about the Regimental Staff 537?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: That was in the Dnieper Castle.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you give us the names of the officers who belonged to the regimental staff?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: At that time there was Lieutenant Colonel Bedenck, the commanding officer; Lieutenant Rex, adjutant; Lieutenant Hodt, orderly officer; and a Captain Schafer, who was a telephone expert. It may be that one or two others were there as well, but I can no longer remember their names.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: The preceding witness has already told us about the tasks of the regimental staff. How were the activities of the regimental staff controlled?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: The regiment, which consisted of 10 to 12 companies, had to give an exact report each evening as to what work had been allotted to the various companies. This was necessary as we had to know what forces were available in case of emergency, for undertaking any new tasks.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How far away from the Dnieper Castle were you billeted?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Approximately 4 to 5 kilometers. I cannot give you the exact distance as I always made it by car, but it would be about 4 to 5 kilometers.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you frequently go to Dnieper Castle?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Very frequently when I was off duty, as I had belonged to this regiment and knew most of the officers, with whom I was on friendly terms.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you tell us about the kind and extent of the traffic to the Dnieper Castle?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: In order to judge this you have to differentiate between persons and things. So far as people were concerned, the traffic was very lively because the regiment had to be very centrally organized in order to be equal to its tasks. Therefore, many couriers came and commanders of the various companies frequently came to visit the regimental staff. On the other hand there was a heavy traffic of trucks and passenger cars, because the regiment tried to improve its billets there; and since we remained there for some time all sorts of building alterations were carried out in the house.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you hear anything about there being three Russian camps with captured Polish officers, 25 to 45 kilometers west of Smolensk, which had allegedly fallen into German hands? VON EICHBORN I never heard anything about any kind of Polish officers' camps or Polish prisoner-of-war camps.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did your army group receive reports about the capture of such Polish officers?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: No. I would have noticed that, since the number of' prisoners, and especially the number of officers, was always submitted to me in the evening reports of the armies which took these prisoners. It was our responsibility to receive these signal reports and we therefore saw them every evening.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: You did not receive a report to that effect?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: I neither saw such a report from an army, which would have issued it, nor did I ever receive a report from an army group which would have had to transmit this report in their evening bulletin to the High Command of the Army (OKH).

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Could a report like that have been handed in from another source or been sent to another office? VON EICHBORN The official channel in the Army was very stringent, and the staffs saw to it that official channels were strictly adhered to. In any case the armies were always required to make the detailed reports, following the lines stipulated in the form sheets and this applied especially to the figures concerning prisoners. Therefore, it is quite out of the question that if such a number of officers had fallen into the hands of an army, it would not have reported the matter through the appropriate channel.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: You said, just a little while ago, that you were in particularly close relationship with the officers of this regiment. Did you ever hear that Polish prisoners of war, officers, were shot at some time or other in the Katyn forest at the instigation of Regiment 537 under Colonel Bedenck or under Colonel Ahrens?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: I knew nearly all the officers of the regiment, as I myself had been over a year with the regiment, and I was on such familiar terms with most of the officers that they told me everything that took place, even anything of an unofficial nature. Therefore, it is quite out of the question that such an important matter should not have come to my knowledge. From the nature of the whole character moulding in the regiment, it is quite impossible that there should not have been at least one who would have come to tell me about it immediately.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were all the operational orders for Regiment 537 officially known to you?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: The operational orders for this army group signal regiment were twofold: The orders which concerned only the wireless company and those which applied to the nine telephone companies. Since I was a telephone expert, it was quite natural for me to draft these orders and submit them to my superior, General Oberhauser. Therefore, each order which was issued had either been drafted by me or I had seen it beforehand. '

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Was there ever at any time an order given out by your office to shoot Polish prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Such an order was neither given to the regiment by our office nor by any other office. Neither did we receive a report to this effect, nor did we hear about things like that through any other channel.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: If an order like that came through official channels, it could come only through you?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: This order would have necessitated a great many members of the regiment being taken away from their own duties, which were to safeguard the system of communications. As we were very short of signallers, we had to know what almost every man in the regiment was doing. It would have been quite out of the question for any member of the regiment to have been taken away from such a duty without our knowledge.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I have no further questions, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Kranzbuhler, whom are you appearing on behalf of?

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUHLER
turns.text: For Grossadmiral Doenitz, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: There is no charge made against Grossadmiral Doenitz in connection with this offense at all.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUHLER
turns.text: Mr. President, the exhumations and the propaganda connected . with them occurred during the period when Grossadmiral Doenitz was Commander-in-Chief of the Navy. The Prosecution alleges that at that time Grossadmiral Doenitz was a member of the Cabinet and had participated in all acts taken by the Government. Therefore, I must consider him as being implicated in all the problems arising out of the Katyn case.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: That would mean that we should have to hear examination from everybody who was connected with the Government. And the Tribunal has already pointed out, with reference to Admiral Raeder, that his case was not connected with this matter. It is only when a case is directly connected with the matter that counsel for the individual defendants are allowed to crossexamine, in addition to the defendant's counsel who calls the witness. If there is any suggestion that you want to make to the counsel who is calling the witness, you can make it to him, but you are not entitled...

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUHLER
turns.text: But I am asking your permission to put two or three questions to this witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: If you have any special questions to put, you may suggest them to Dr. Stahmer, and Dr. Stahmer will put them. Dr. Kranzbuhler, if you want to put any questions, you may put them to Dr. Stahmer, and he will put them to the witness.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUHLER
turns.text: Mr. President, I did not quite understand. Shall I propose to Dr. Stahmer to put the questions or...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: If you cannot do it verbally, you may do it in writing; and you may do it later on. But I really do not think there can be any questions which are so difficult to suggest to Dr. Stahmer as all that.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUHLER
turns.text: They can also be put through Dr. Stahmer. I was only thinking that I would save some time by putting the questions myself.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I told you if you wish to ask any questions, you must ask them through Dr. Stahmer.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUHLER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: In the meantime, the Tribunal will go on with the cross-examination, and any questions which you wish to put can be put in re-examination. Does the Prosecution wish to cross-examine?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Witness, I am interested to know your exact function in the army. Were you in charge of teleprinter communications at the headquarters of Army Group' Center or were you a wireless expert?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: No, Mr. Prosecutor, you are wrong. I was the telephone expert of Army Group Center, not the wireless expert. ~

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: That is exactly what I am asking you. The translation was evidently incorrect. So you were in charge of telephone communications, were you not?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Yes; you are right.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Ordinary telegrams, or ciphered telegrams?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: The task of a telephone expert connected with an army group consisted in keeping the telephone lines intact . . .

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: No, I am not interested in the tasks in a general way. I would like to know whether these were secret ciphered telegrams or the ordinary army mail, army communications which were not secret.

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: There were two kinds of telegrams, open and secret.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Were secret telegrams transmitted by you, too?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Both came through me.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Consequently, all communications between the Wehrmacht, between Army units and the highest police authorities also passed through you; is that correct?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: The most important telegrams, and especially the secret ones were submitted to the telephone expert.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes. Consequently, the correspondence between the police authorities and the Armed Forces units passed through you; is that correct? I am asking you this question for a second time.

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: I must answer with the reservation that the messages did not pass through the telephone expert, but only the most important secret teletype matters were submitted to him- not the whole correspondence, because that went also through the mail as well as by courier service.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: That is clear. Do you know in this case that in September and October 1941 there were special detachments in Smolensk whose duty, in close co-operation with the Army, was to carry out the so-called purge of the prisoner-of-war camps and the extermination of prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: DR. LATERNSER
turns.text: Mr. President, I must decisively object to this questioning of the witness. This questioning can have only the purpose of determining the relations between the General Staff and the OKW and any commands of the Security Service. Therefore, they are accusing the General Staff and the OKW; and if I, Mr. President, as defense counsel for the General Staff and the OKW am not permitted to put questions, then on the basis of equal treatment, the same rules must apply to the Prosecution as well.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: May I, Mr. President, make a short statement?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Smirnov, the question is competent.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I beg your pardon.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I said the question was competent. You may ask the question.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I would like to ask you the following question, Witness. Since all secret teletypes passed through you, did you ever encounter among. these telegrams any from the so-called 1st Einsatzgruppe "B"-that was the so-called first command-or from the Special Command "Moscow" which at that time was located at Smolensk and kept in reserve in anticipation of better times? The latter had the order to perpetrate mass murders in Moscow. Both commands were located at Smolensk at that time.

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: No such reports came into my hands. I can fully explain this to you, Mr. Prosecutor. When any detachments of this sort had been established in the area of Army Group Center, these detachments had their own wireless stations. It was only later on in the course of the Russian campaign that these posts had teletype facilities as well; then they used the army group network. However, that only happened later.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Consequently, the telegrams of those special units which, by order of high police authorities, were assigned to carry out special actions in co-operation with military units, did not pass through your hands in September and October of 1941?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: That is correct. At that time, there were no teletype facilities and offices for such special units, even if they were in that area at all.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Mr. President, this document was already presented to the Court together with the Extraordinary State Commission Report, Document Number USSR-3. If the High Tribunal will permit it, I should like to present to the Tribunal and to the Defense photostatic copies of one of the documents which was attached to the report of the Extraordinary State Commission. If the Tribunal will look at Page 2 of this document, it will see that the Special Command "Moscow" and the Einsatzgruppe "B" were both located in Smolensk. It says on the first page that these detachments together with units of the Armed Forces, were assigned to carry out mass killings in the camps. If the Tribunal will permit me, I shall submit this document now...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Smirnov, that is a matter of argument. We shall take judicial notice of it, of course, of everything which is in the Soviet Government's publication. And I understand you to say that this document is a part of the Soviet Government communication or Soviet Government report.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, Mr. President; but I would like to ask permission to present an original German document, a secret document, which states that in the Smolensk area there were two large special commands whose duties were to carry out mass murders in the camps, and that these actions had to be carried out together with the Armed Forces units which had to co-operate with them.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Smirnov, is this document which you have just handed up to us a part of the report USSR-3?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, Mr. President, it is a part of the report, Document USSR-3, called "Special Directives of the Hitler Government Concerning the Annihilation of Prisoners of War." I would like to ask the Tribunal to allow me to present one of the original documents even if the report, USSR-3, has been already submitted in full. It says there that these special units were located in Smolensk and were assigned together with the Armed Forces units to carry out mass killings in the camps.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, Colonel Smirnov. This' document is already in evidence, if the Tribunal understands correctly.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. President. [Turning to the witness.] Consequently, we may consider it as an established fact that the correspondence, the telegraphic messages of these special detachments did not pass through your hands; is that correct?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: He has said that twice already.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Excuse me, Mr. President. [Turning to the witness.] Why did you assert with such certainty that there were no reports about the killing of the Poles? You know that the killing of the Polish prisoners of war was a special action, and any report about this action would have to pass through your hands? Is that correct?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: I answered the prosecutor-rather, I answered Dr. Stahmer-that if in the area of Army Group Signal Regiment 537 killings of that sort had taken place, I would undoubtedly have known about them. I did not state what the prosecutor is now trying to ascribe to me.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Smirnov, the Tribunal think you had better read this passage from this document, which is in the German language, to the Tribunal so that it will go into the record.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: In this document, Mr. President, it is stated...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Go on, Colonel Smirnov.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. President. This document is dated "Berlin, 29 October 1941." It is headed, "The Chief of the Security Police and of the Security Service." It has a classification, "Top Secret; Urgent letter; Operational Order Number-14." Reference is made to decrees of 17 July and 12 Sep{ember 1941. I shall now read a few short sentences, and I shall begin with the first sentence: "In the appendix, I am sending directions for the evacuation of Soviet civilian prisoners and prisoners of war out of permanent prisoner-of-war camps and transit camps in the rear of the Army... "These directives have been worked out in collaboration with the Army High Command. The Army High Command-has notified the commanders of the armies in the rear as well as the local commanders of the prisoner-of-war camps and of the transit camps. "The task force groups, depending on the size of the camp in their territory, are setting up special commands in sufficient strength under the leadership of an SS leader. The commands are instructed immediately to start work in the camps." I break off here, and will continue reading the last paragraph: "I emphasize especially that Operational Orders Number 8 and 14 as well as the appendix are to be destroyed immediately in the case of immediate danger." I shall finish my reading and now I shall only mention the distribution list. On Page 2 I quote the part concerning Smolensk. It says here that in Smolensk the Einsatzgruppe "B" was located, consisting of Special Commands 7a, 7b, 8, and 9; and in addition to this, there was already located in Smolensk a special command, which had been rather prematurely named "Moscow" by its organizers. These are the contents of the document, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal directs that the whole document shall be translated. We will now recess until 5 minutes past 2 o'clock. [The Tribunal recessed until 1405 hours.] 307, Afternoon Session

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Mr. President, I have no more questions to put to this witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Stahmer.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Witness, do you know who owned that little castle near the Dnieper before the occupation by German troops? Who owned it, who lived there?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: I cannot say that for certain. We noticed that the little castle was astonishingly well furnished. It was very well laid out. It had two bathrooms, a rifle range, and a cinema. We drew certain conclusions therefrom, when the facts became known, but I do not know anything about the previous owner.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: The Russian Prosecutor submitted to you a document dated 29 October 1941, "Directives to the Chief of the Sipo for the Detachments in the Stalags." With reference to that document, I want to ask you whether you had an opportunity personally to ascertain the attitude of Field Marshal Kluge, your commander of Army Group Center, regarding the shooting of prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: By chance I became the ear-witness of a conversation between the Commanders Bock and Kluge. That conversation took place about 3 or 4 weeks before the beginning of the Russian campaign. I cannot tell you the exact time. At the time Field Marshal Von Bock was the commander of Army Group Center, and Field Marshal Von Kluge was commander of the 4th Army. The army group was in Posen and the 4th Army at Warsaw. One day I was called by the aide-de-camp of Field Marshal Von Bock, who was Lieutenant Colonel Count Hardenberg. He gave me the order...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: These details are entirely irrelevant, aren't they. All you want to ask him is: What was the attitude of Von Kluge? That is all.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: The answer did not come through. I did not understand what you said, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What I said was that all these details about the particular place where Von Kluge met some other army group commander are utterly irrelevant. All you are trying to ask him is: What was Von Kluge's attitude toward the murder of war prisoners? Isn't that all?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Yes. [Turning to the witness.] Will you answer the question briefly, Witness. Please just tell us what Von Kluge said.

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: Von Kluge told Von Bock, during a telephone conversation, that the order for the shooting of certain prisoners of war was an impossibility and could not be carried out, with regard to the discipline of the troops. Von Bock shared this point of view and both these gentlemen talked for half an hour about the measures which they wanted to adopt against this order.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: According to the allegations of the Prosecution, the shooting of these 11,000 Polish officers is supposed to have been carried out sometime in September 1941. The question now is: Do you consider it possible, in view of local conditions, that such mass shootings and burials could have been carried out next door to the regimental headquarters without you yourself having heard about it?

turns.speaker: VON EICHBORN
turns.text: We were very busy in preparation for the move of the army group to Smolensk. We had assigned a great number of signal troops for setting up perfect installations. On the entire site there was a constant going and coming of troops laying cables and telephone lines. It is out of the question that anything of this kind could have occurred in that particular area without the regiment and I getting knowledge of it.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I have no further questions to put to the witness, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The witness can retire.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Mr. President, before calling my third witness, Lieutenant General Oberhauser, may I ask your permission to make the following remarks? The Prosecution has up to now only alleged that Regiment Hum: her 537 was the one which had carried out these shootings and that under Colonel Ahrens' command. Today again, Colonel Ahrens has been named by the Prosecution as being the perpetrator. Apparently this allegation has been dropped and it has been said that if it was not Ahrens then it must have been his predecessor, Colonel Bedenck; and if Colonel Bedenck did not do it, then apparently-and this seems to be the third version-it was done by the SD. The Defense had taken the position solely that Colonel Ahrens was accused as the perpetrator and it has refuted that allegation. Considering the changed situation and the attitude adopted by the Prosecution, I shall have to name a fourth witness in addition. That is First Lieutenant Eodt, who has been mentioned today as the perpetrator and who was with the regimental staff right from the beginning and who was, as we have told, the senior of the advance party which arrived at the Dnieper Castle in July. I got the address of First Lieutenant Hodt by chance yesterday. He is at Glucksburg near Flensburg; and I, therefore, ask to be allowed to name as a witness First Lieutenant Hodt, who will give evidence that during the time between July and September such shootings did not occur.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Stahmer, the Tribunal will consider your application, when they adjourn at half past 3, with reference to this extra witness.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Yes, Sir. Then I shall now call Lieutenant General Oberhauser as witness. [The witness Oberhauser took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you state your full name, please?

turns.speaker: EUGEN OBERHAUSER (Witness)
turns.text: Eugen Oberhauser.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear by God-the Almighty and Omniscient-that I will speak the pure truth-and will withhold and add nothing. [The witness repeated the oath.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You may sit down.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: General, what position did you hold during the war?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I was the signal commander in an army group, first of all during the Polish campaign, in Army Group North; then, in the Western campaign Army Group B; and then in Russia, Army Group Center.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: When did you and your staff reach the neighborhood of Katyn?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Sometime during September 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Where was your staff located?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: My staff was located in the immediate vicinity of the commander of the army group; that is to say, about 12 kilometers west of Smolensk, near-the railroad station of Krasnibor.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Was Regiment Number 537 under your command?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Regiment 537 was directly under my command.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What task did that regiment have?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: That regiment had the task of establishing both telegraph and wireless communications between-the command of the army group and the various armies and other units which were directly under its command.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Was the staff of the regiment stationed near you?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: The staff of that regiment was located about 3, perhaps 4 kilometers west from my own position.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you give us more detailed information regarding the exact location of the staff headquarters of Number 537?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: The staff headquarters of 537 was in a very nice Russian timber house. Commissars were supposed to have been living there before. It was on the steep' bank of the Dnieper River. It was somewhat off the road, perhaps 400 to 500 meters away. It was, from my place, 4 kilometers west of the main highway Smolensk to Vitebsk.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Who was the commanding officer of the regiment after the capture of Smolensk?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: After the capture of Smolensk, Colonel Bedenck was the commander of the regiment.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: For how long?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Until about November 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Who was his successor?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: His successor was Colonel Ahrens.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How long?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Approximately until September-it may have been August - 1943.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were you near Katyn as long as that, too?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I was there until the command of the army group transferred its headquarters farther west.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What were your relations with the commanders of this regiment?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: My relations with the regimental commanders were most hearty, both officially and privately, which is due to the fact that I had been the first commander of that regiment. I myself had formed the regiment and I was most attached to it.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you personally visit the little Dnieper Castle frequently? '

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I went to the Dnieper Castle frequently; I can well say in normal times once or twice a week.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did the commanders visit you in the meantime?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: The commanders came to see me more frequently than I went to see them

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you know anything about the fact that near Smolensk, about 25 to 45 kilometers to the west, there were three Russian camps which contained Polish prisoners of war...

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I knew nothing of that.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: . . . who had fallen into the hands of the Germans?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I never heard anything about it.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Was there an order, which is supposed to have come from Berlin, that Polish officers who were prisoners of war were to be shot?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: No, such an order was never issued.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you yourself ever give such an order?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I have never given such an order.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know whether Colonel Bedenck or Colonel Ahrens ever caused such shootings to be carried out?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I am not informed, but I consider it absolutely impossible.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Why?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: First, because such a decisive order would necessarily have gone through me, for I was the direct superior of the regiment; and second, because if such an order had been given, for a reason which I could not understand, and transmitted to the regiment through some obscure channel, then the commanders would most certainly have rung me up or they would have come to see me and said, "General, they are asking something here which we cannot understands'

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know First Lieutenant Hodt?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Yes, I know him.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What position did he have in Regiment 537?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Hodt held various posts in the regiment. Usually, he was sent ahead because he was a particularly qualified officer-especially in regard to technical qualifications-in order to make preparations when headquarters was being changed. He was therefore used as advance party of the so-called technical company in order to establish the new command posts; and then he was the regimental expert for the telephone system, dealing with all matters relating to the telephone and teletype system with the command headquarters of the army group. In my staff he was occasionally detailed to fill the positions of any of my officers when they were on leave.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Was he also in charge of the advance party during the advance on Katyn?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: That I cannot say. I can only say that I personally heard from my staff signal commander that he had sent an officer ahead, after it had been ascertained how the headquarters were to be laid out, that this officer was acting on my behalf, as at the time I still remained in the old quarters, and he was preparing things in the way I wanted them from the point of view of the signal commander. I do not know who was in charge of that advance party at the time, but it is quite possible that it was First Lieutenant Hodt.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were you in Katyn or the vicinity during the period after the capture of Smolensk, which was, I believe, on or about 20 July 1941, and up to the transfer of your staff to Katyn on 20 September?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I was in the vicinity. I was where the headquarters of the army group wanted to settle down; that is, in the woods west of Smolensk, where Katyn is located.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were you frequently there during that time?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I should say three or four times.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you talk to Hodt on those occasions?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: If he was the officer in charge of the advance party, which I cannot say today, then I must certainly have talked to him. At any rate, I did talk to the officer whom I had sent ahead and also to the one from my regiment.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you hear anything about shootings occurring during that time?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I heard nothing, nor did I hear anything at all except in 1943, when the graves were opened.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you or Regiment 537 have the necessary technical means, pistols, ammunition, and so on, at your disposal which would have made it possible to carry out shootings on such a scale?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: The regiment, being a signal regiment in the rear area, was not equipped with weapons and ammunition as well as the actual fighting troops. Such a task, however, would have been something unusual for the regiment; first, because a signal regiment has completely different tasks, and secondly it would not have been in a position technically to carry out such mass executions.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know the place where these graves were discovered later on?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I know the site because I drove past it a great deal.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you describe it more accurately?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Taking the main road Smolensk-Vitebsk, a path led through wooded undulating ground. There were sandy spaces, which were, however, covered with scrub and heather, and along that narrow path one got to the Dnieper Castle from the main road.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were the places where these graves were later discovered already overgrown when you got there?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: They were overgrown just like the surrounding ground, and there was no difference between them and the rest of the surroundings.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: In view of your knowledge of the place, would you consider it possible that 11,000 Poles could have been buried at that spot, people who may have been shot between June and September 1941?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I consider that it is out of the question, for the mere reason that if the commander had known it at the time he would certainly never have chosen this spot for his headquarters, next to 11,000 dead.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you tell me how the graves were discovered?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Officially I had nothing to do with that. I only heard that through local inhabitants or somebody else it had become known that large-scale executions had taken place there years ago.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: From whom did you hear that?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Quite probably from the commander himself, who, because he was located on the spot, had heard more about it than I had. But I cannot remember exactly now.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: So you did not receive official notice about the discovery of the graves, did you?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: No, I never did.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: After the opening of the graves, did you talk to the German or foreign members of the commission?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I have never talked to any members of that commission.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I have no further questions, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Smirnov.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Witness, you arrived in the region of Katyn in September 1943?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Excuse me, I meant September 1941. Is that correct?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Yes, September 1941.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And you contend that you did not know anything either about the camps for Polish prisoners of war or the prisoners in the hands of the German troops, is that so?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I have never heard anything about Polish prisoners of war being in the hands of German troops.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I understand that this had no relation to your official activity as the commander of a signal regiment. But in spite of this you may perhaps have witnessed that various German troops combed the woods in the vicinity of the Smolensk-Vitebsk highway to capture Polish prisoners of war who had escaped from the camps?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I never heard anything about troops going there in order to, shall we say, recapture escaped Polish prisoners of war. I am hearing this here for the first time.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please answer me. Have you perhaps seen German military units escorting Polish prisoners of war who were captured in the woods?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I have not seen that.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please answer the following question: You were on good terms with Colonel Ahrens, were you not?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I have had good relations with all commanders of the regiment.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And in addition to that, you were his immediate superior?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Right.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Colonel Ahrens found out about the mass graves at the end of 1941 or at the beginning of 1942. Did he tell you anything about his discovery?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I cannot believe that Colonel Ahrens could have discovered the graves in 1941. I cannot imagine that-I especially cannot imagine that he would tell me nothing about it.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: In any case do you contend that neither in 1942 nor in 1943 did Colonel Ahrens report to you in regard to this affair? I

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Colonel Ahrens never told me anything about it, and he would have told me if he had known.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I am interested in the following answer which you gave to a question by defense counsel. You remarked that the signal regiment had not enough weapons to carry out shootings. What do you mean by that? How many, and what kind of weapons did the regiment possess?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: The signal regiment were mostly equipped with pistols and with carbines. They had no automatic arms.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Pistols? Of what caliber?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: They were Parabellum pistols. The caliber, I think, was 7.65, but I cannot remember for certain.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Parabellum pistols, 7.65, or were there Mauser pistols or any other kind of weapons?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: That varied. Noncommissioned officers, as far as I know, had the smaller Mauser pistols. Actuary, only noncommissioned officers were equipped with pistols. The majority of the men had carbines.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I would like you to tell us some more about the pistols. You say that they were 7.65 caliber pistols, is that so?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I cannot now, at the moment, give you exact information about the caliber. I only know that the Parabellum pistol was 7.65 or some such caliber. I think the Mauser pistol had a somewhat smaller caliber.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And Walter pistols?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: There were also Walters. I think they had the same caliber as the Mauser. It is a smaller, black pistol; and it is better than the somewhat cumbersome Parabellum pistol which is heavier.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, that is quite correct. Please tell me whether in this regiment the noncommissioned officers possessed those small pistols.

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: As a rule, noncommissioned officers had pistols but not carbines.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I see. Perhaps you can tell us about how many pistols this signal regiment possessed?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Of course I cannot tell you that now. Let us assume that every noncommissioned officer had a pistol...

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: And how many noncommissioned officers were there? How many pistols in all were there in your regiment if you consider that every noncommissioned officer had a pistol?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Assuming that every noncommissioned officer in the regiment had a pistol that would amount to 15 per company, a total of 150. However, to give a definite statement about that figure retrospectively now is impossible. I can only give you clues.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Why do you consider that 150 pistols would be insufficient to carry out these mass killings which went on over a period of time? What makes you so positive about that?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Because a signal regiment of an army group deployed over a large area as in the case of Army Group Center is never together as a unit. The regiment was spread out from Kolodov l July 46 as far as Vitebsk, and there were small detachments everywhere, and in the headquarters of the regiment there were comparatively few people; in other words, there were never 150 pistols in one and the same place.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: The main part of the signal regiment was located in the Katyn woods,' was it not?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I did not understand your question.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: The main portions of your regiment were located in the Katyn woods, were they not?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: The first company was mainly located between the regimental staff quarters and the actual command post of the army group. That was the company which was handling the communications, the telephone and teleprinted communications for the army group. It was the company, therefore, which was nearest.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: One more question. The officers of your regiment were obviously armed with pistols and not with carbines?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Officers had pistols only, and as a rule they only had small ones. Possibly one or the other may have had a Parabellum pistol.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: That is to say either a Walter or a Mauser?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Did you frequently visit the villa where the headquarters of Regiment 537 was located?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Yes, I was there at least once, sometimes twice, a week.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Were you ever interested as to why soldiers from other military units visited the villa in Kozy Gory and why special beds were prepared for them as well as drinks and food?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I cannot imagine that there were any largescale visits of other soldiers or members of other units. I do not know anything about that.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I am not speaking about a great number. I am speaking of 20 or sometimes 25 men.

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: If the regimental commander summoned his company and detachment) commanders for an officers' meeting, then, of course, there would be a few dozen of such officers who normally would not be seen there.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: No, I am not talking about officers who belonged to the unit. I would like to ask you another somewhat different question. Would the number 537 appear on the shoulder straps of the soldiers belonging to that regiment?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: As far as I recollect the number was on the shoulder straps, but at the beginning of the war it could be concealed by a camouflage flap. I cannot remember whether during that particular period these covers were used or not.
