At any rate at the street entrance to the regimental headquarters there was a black-yellow-black flag, which bore the number 537.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I am speaking of soldiers who came to the villa in Kozy Gory, and who did not have the number 537 on their shoulder straps. Were you ever interested in finding out what those soldiers did there in September and October of 1941? Did the commander of the unit report to you about this?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: May I ask what year this was supposed to be, 1941?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Yes, 1941, that is the year which is concerned.

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I do not think that at that time there was much coming and going of outsiders at staff headquarters because during that period everything was in course of construction and I cannot imagine that other units, even small groups of 20 or 25 people should have been there. I personally, as I have told you, was there only once or twice weekly, and not before September or October.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Beginning with what date of September did you start visiting there? You said it was in September but not from what date.

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I cannot tell you. The commander of the army group moved at the end of September from Borossilov, shortly before the battle of Vyazma, which was on 2 October, into that district.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Consequently, you could start visiting this villa for instance only at the end of September or the beginning of October 1941?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: It was only then that the little castle was finally occupied, for the regiment did not arrive much earlier than we from the command of the army group. low PRESIDENT: Colonel Smirnov, is it necessary to go into this detail? Have you any particular purpose in going into so much detail? I'

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Mr. President, I ask this question for the following reasons: Later we shall interrogate witnesses for the Soviet Prosecution on the same point and particularly the chief of the medico-legal investigation. That is why I would like to ask the permission of the Court to clarify this point concerning the time when the witness visited the villa. That will be my last question to this point.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, very well. Do not go into greater detail than you find absolutely necessary.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Consequently, at the beginning of September and the first part of October 1941 you were not in the villa of Katyn woods and you could not be there at the time, is that true?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I cannot remember that exactly. The regimental commander had spotted the little castle and set it up for his staff headquarters. When exactly he moved in I cannot know, because I had other jobs to do.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: No, I asked whether you personally could not have been in the viva during the first part of September. Could you not possibly have been there before 20 September?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I do not think so.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I have no further questions, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do you wish to re-examine, Dr. Stahmer?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Unfortunately, Mr. President, I shall have to come back to the question of time because it was not brought out too clearly during these last questions. When did Regiment 537 move into the castle?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I assume it was during September.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Beginning or end of September?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Probably rather more toward the end of September.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Until then only the advance party was there, or . . .

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: The advance party of the regiment was there and my officers whom I had sent ahead.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How many noncommissioned officers were with the advance party?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I cannot tell you exactly how many the regiment sent. I personally had sent one officer. Generally the regiment could not have sent very many. As a rule, as is always the case, the regiment was still operating at the old command post in Borossilov and simultaneously it had to set up the new post. Consequently, during this period of regrouping, on the point of moving a command of an army group, there is always a considerable shortage of men. The old headquarters still has to be looked after, the new post requires men for its construction, so that as always during this period there were certainly too few people.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you not even give us an estimate of the figure of that advance party?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: There were 30, 40, or 50 men.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How many noncommissioned officers?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Probably one or two officers, a few noncommissioned officers, and some men.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: The regiment was very widely spread out, was it not?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How far, approximately? OBERHAUSER- In the entire area of Army Group Center, shall we say between Orel and Vitebsk-in that entire area they were widely dispersed.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How many kilometers was that, approximately?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: More than 500 kilometers.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know Judge Advocate General Dr. Konrad of Army Group Center?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know whether, in 1943, he interrogated the local inhabitants under oath about the date when the Polish officers were supposed to have been shot in the woods of Katyn?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: No, I do not know.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I have no further questions, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Were there any Einsatzkommandos in the Katyn area during the time that you were there?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Nothing has ever come to my knowledge about that.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Did you ever hear of an order to shoot Soviet commissars?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I only knew of that by hearsay.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: When?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Probably at the beginning of the Russian campaign, I think. 1 July AS

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Before the campaign started or after?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: I cannot remember having heard anything like that before the beginning of the campaign.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Who was to carry out that order?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Strictly speaking, signal troops are not really fighting troops. Therefore, they really had nothing to do with that at all, and therefore we were in no way affected by the order.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I did not ask you that. I asked you who had to carry out the order.

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: Those who came into contact with these people, presumably.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Anybody who came in contact with Russian commissars had to kill them; is that it?

turns.speaker: OBERHAUSER
turns.text: No, I assume that it was the troops, the fighting troops, the actual fighting troops at the front who first met the enemy. That could only have applied to the army group. The signal regiment never came into a position to meet commissars. That is probably why they were not mentioned in the order or affected by it in any way.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The witness can retire.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Mr. President, I ask permission to call as witness the former deputy mayor of the city of Smolensk during the German occupation, Professor of Astronomy, Boris Bazilevsky.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, let him come in then. [The witness Bazilevsky took the stand.] Will you state your full name, please'

turns.speaker: BORIS BAZILEVSKY (Witness)
turns.text: Boris Bazilevsky.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you make this form of oath: I, a citizen of the USSR-called as a witness in this case-solemnly promise and swear before the High Tribunal-to say all that I know about this case-and to add or to withhold nothing. [The witness repeated the oath.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You may sit down.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: With the permission of the Tribunal, I should like to start with my interrogation, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please tell us, Witness, what your activity was before the German occupation of the city and district of Smolensk and where you were living in Smolensk.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Before the occupation of Smolensk and the surrounding region...

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please speak slowly.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I lived in the city of Smolensk and was professor first at the Smolensk University and then of the Smolensk Pedagogical Institute, and at the same time I was director of the Smolensk Astronomical Observatory. For 10 years I was the dean of the physics and mathematics faculty, and in the last years I was deputy to the director of the scientific department of the Institute.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: How many years did you live in Smolensk previous to the German occupation?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: From 1919.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Do you know what the so-called Katyn wood was?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes. '

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please speak slowly.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Actually, it was a grove. It was the favorite resort of the inhabitants of Smolensk who spent their holidays and vacations there.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Was this wood before the war a special reservation which was fenced or guarded by armed patrols, by watch dogs?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: During the many years that I lived in Smolensk, this place was never fenced; and no restrictions were ever placed on access to it. I personally used to go there very frequently. The last time I was there was in 1940 and in the spring of 1941. In this wood there was also a camp for engineers. Thus, there was free access to this place for everybody.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please tell me in what year there was an engineer camp?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: As far as I know, it was there for many years.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please speak slowly.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Wait a minute. Professor, will you wait a minute, please? When you see that yellow light go on, it means that you are going too fast; and when you are asked a question, will you pause before you answer it? Do you understand?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Will you please repeat your answer, and very slowly, if you please.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: The last time I know that the engineer camp was in the area of the Katyn wood was in 1941.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Consequently, if I understand you correctly, in 1940 and 1941 before the beginning of the war at any rate-and you speak of the spring of 1941-the Katynwood was not a special reservation and was accessible to everybody?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes. I say that that was the situation.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Do you say this as an eyewitness or from hearsay?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: No, I say it as an eyewitness who used to go there frequently.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please tell the Tribunal under what circumstances you became the first deputy mayor of Smolensk during the period of the German occupation. Please speak slowly.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I was an administration official; and I did not have an opportunity of leaving the place in time, because I was busy in saving the particularly precious library of the Institute and the very valuable equipment. In the circumstances I could not try to escape before the evening of the 15th, but then I did not succeed in catching the train. I therefore decided to leave the city on 16 July in the morning, but during the night of 15 to 16 the city was unexpectedly occupied by German troops. All the bridges across the Dnieper were blown up, and I found myself in captivity. After some time, on 20 July, a group of German soldiers came to the observatory of which I was the director. They took down that I was the director and that I was living there and that there was also a professor of physics, Efimov, living in the same building. In the evening of 20 July two German officers came to me and brought me to the headquarters of the unit which had occupied Smolensk. After checking my personalia and after a short conversation, they suggested that I become mayor of the city. I refused, basing my refusal on the fact that I was a professor of astronomy and that, as I had no experience in such matters, I could not undertake this post. They then declared categorically and with threats, "We are going to force the Russian intelligentsia to work."

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Thus, if I understand you correctly, the Germans forced you by threats to become the deputy mayor of Smolensk?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: That is not all. They told me also that in a few days I would be summoned to the Kommandantur. On 25 July a man in civilian clothes appeared at my apartment, accompanied by a German policeman, and represented himself as a lawyer, Menschagin. He declared that he came by order of the military headquarters and that I should accompany him immediately to headquarters.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You are spending a lot of time on how he came to be mayor of Smolensk.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Will you please allow me to pass to other questions, Mr. President? Thank you for your observations. [Turning to the witness.] Who was your immediate superior? Who was the mayor of Smolensk?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Menschagin.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: What were the relations between this man and the German administration and particularly with the German Kommandantur?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: These relations were very good and became closer and closer every day.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Is it correct to say that Menschagin was the trustee of the German administration and that they even gave him secret information?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Do you know that in the vicinity of Smolensk there were Polish prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes, I do very well.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Do you know what they were doing?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I do not know what this is going to prove. You presumably do, but can you not come nearer to the point?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: He said that he knew there were Polish prisoners of war in Smolensk; and, with the permission of the Tribunal, I would like to ask the witness what these prisoners of war were doing.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well; go on.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please answer. What were the Polish prisoners of war doing in the vicinity of Smolensk, and at what time?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: In the spring of 1941 and at the beginning of the summer they were working on the restoration of the roads, Moscow-Minsk and Smolensk-Vitebsk.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: What do you know about the further fate of the Polish prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Thanks to the position that I occupied, I learned very early about the fate of the Polish prisoners of war.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please tell the Tribunal what you know about it.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: In the camp for Russian prisoners of war known as "Dulag 126" there prevailed such a severe regime that prisoners of war were dying by the hundreds every day; for this reason I tried to free all those from this camp for whose release a reason could be given. I learned that in this camp there was also a very well-known pedagogue named Zhiglinski. I asked Menschagin to make representations to the German Kommandantur of Smolensk, and in particular to Von Schwetz, and to plead for the release of Zhiglinski from this camp.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please do not go into detail and do not waste time, but tell the Tribunal about your conversation with Menschagin. What did he tell you?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Menschagin answered my request with, "What is the use? We can save one, but hundreds will die." However, I insisted; and Menschagin. after some hesitation, agreed to put this request to the German Kommandantur.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please be short and tell us what Menschagin told you when he came back from the German Kommandantur.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Two days later he told me that he was in a very difficult situation on account of my demand. Von Schwetz had refused the request by referring to an instruction from Berlin saying that a very severe regime should prevail with respect to prisoners of war.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: What did he tell you about Polish prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: As to Polish prisoners of war, he told me that Russians would at least be allowed to die in the camps while there were proposals to exterminate the Poles.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: What else was said?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I replied, "What do you mean? What do you want to say? How do you understand this?" And Menschagin answered, "You should understand this in the very literal sense of these words." He asked me not to tell anybody about it, since it was a great secret.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: When did this conversation of yours take place with Menschagin? In what month, and on what day?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: This conversation took place at the beginning of September. I cannot remember the exact date.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: But you remember it was the beginning of September?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Did you ever come back again to the fate of Polish prisoners of war in your further conversations with Menschagin?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Can you tell us when?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Two weeks later-that is to say, at the end of September-I could not help asking him, "What was the fate of the Polish prisoners of war?" At first Menschagin hesitated, and then he told me haltingly, "They have already died. It is all over for them."

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Did he tell you where they were killed?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: He told me that they had been shot in the vicinity of Smolensk, as Von Schwetz told him.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Did he mention the exact place?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: No, he did not mention the exact place.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Tell me this. Did you, in turn, tell anybody about the extermination, by Hitlerites, of the Polish prisoners of war near Smolensk?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I talked about this to Professor Efimov, who was living in the same house with me. Besides him, a few days later I had a conversation about it with Dr. Nikolski, who was the medical officer of the city. However, I found out that Nikolski knew about this crime already from some other source.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Did Menschagin tell you why these shootings took place?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes. When he told me that the prisoners of war had been killed, he emphasized once more the necessity of keeping it strictly secret in order to avoid disagreeable consequences. He started to explain to me the reasons for the German behavior with respect to the Polish prisoners of war. He pointed out that this was only one measure of the general system of treating Polish prisoners of war.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Did you hear anything about the extermination of the Poles from the employees of the German Kommandantur?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes, 2 or 3 days later.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You are both going too fast, and you are not pausing enough. You are putting your questions whilst the answers are coming through. You must have longer pauses, and go slower.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. President. [Turning to the witness.] Please continue, but slowly.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I do not know where I was.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I asked you whether any of the employees of the German Kommandantur told you anything about the extermination of the Poles.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Two or three days later, when I visited the of lice of Menschagin, I met there an interpreter, the Sonderfuehrer of the 7th Division of the German Kommandantur who was in charge of the Russian administration and who had a conversation with Menschagin concerning the Poles. He came from the Baltic region.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Perhaps you can tell us briefly what he said.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: When I entered the room he was saying, "The Poles are a useless people, and exterminated they may serve as fertilizer and for the enlargement of living space for the German nation."

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You are doing exactly what I said just now. You are asking the questions before the translation comes through.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Excuse me, Mr. President, I will try to speak more slowly. [Turning to the witness.] Did you learn from Menschagin anything definite about the shooting of Polish prisoners of war?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: When I entered the room I heard the conversation with Hirschfeld. I missed the beginning, but from the context of the conversation it was clear that they spoke about this event.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Did Menschagin, when telling you about the shooting of Polish prisoners of war, refer to Von Schwetz?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes; I had the impression that he referred to Von Schwetz. But evidently-and this is my firm belief-he also spoke about it with private persons in the Kommandantur.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: When did Menschagin tell you that Polish prisoners of war were killed near Smolensk?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: It was at the end of September.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I have no further questions to put to this witness, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal will adjourn. [A recess was taken.]

turns.speaker: MARSHAL
turns.text: If it please the Tribunal, the Defendant Hess is absent.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Stahmer.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Witness, in your testimony, just before recess, you read out your testimony, if I observed correctly. Will you tell me whether that was so or not?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I was not reading anything. I have only a plan of the courtroom in my hand.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: It looked to me as though you were reading out your answers. How can you explain the fact that the interpreter already had your answer in his hands?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I do not know how the interpreters could have had my answers beforehand. The testimony which I am giving was, however, known to the Commission beforehand-that is, my testimony during the preliminary examination.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know the little castle on the Dnieper, the little villa? Did you not understand me or hear me? Do you know the little castle on the Dnieper, the little villa on the Dnieper?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I do not know which villa you mean. There were quite a number of villas on the Dnieper.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: The house which was near the Katyn wood on the steep bank of the Dnieper River.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I still do not quite understand which house you mean. The banks of the Dnieper are long, and therefore your question is quite incomprehensible to me.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know where the graves of Katyn were found, in which 11,000 Polish officers were buried?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I was not there. I did not see the Katyn burial grounds.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Had you never been in the Katyn wood?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: As I already said, I was there not once but many times. -

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know where this mass burial site was located?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: How can I know where the burial grounds were situated when I could not go there since the occupation?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How do you know that the little wood was not fenced in? '

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Before the occupation of the Smolensk district by the German troops, the entire area, -as I already stated, was not surrounded by any barrier; but according to hearsay I knew that after the occupation access to this wood was prohibited by the German local command.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Therefore you have no knowledge of the fact that here in the Katyn wood a sanitarium or a convalescent home of the GPU was located?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I know very well; that was known to all the citizens of Smolensk.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Then, of course, you also know exactly which house I referred to in my question?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I, myself, had never been in that house. In general, access to that house was only allowed to the families of the employees of the Ministry of the Interior. As to other persons, there was no need and no facility for them to go there.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: The house, therefore, was closed off?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: No, the house was not forbidden to strangers; but why should people go there if they had no business there or were not in the sanitarium? The garden, of course, was open to the public.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Were there not guards stationed there?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I have never seen any.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Is this Russian witness who reported to you about the matter concerning the Polish officers, is this witness still alive?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Mr. Counsel, you probably mean Mayor Menschagin, if I understand you rightly?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: When you read your testimony off, it was not easy for me to follow. What was the mayor's name? Menschagin? Is he still alive?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Menschagin went away together with the German troops during their retreat, and I remained, and Menschagin's fate is unknown to me.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Stahmer, you are not entitled to say to the witness, 'when you read your testimony off," just now, because he denied that he read his testimony off and there is no evidence that he has read it off.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did this Russian witness tell you that the Polish officers had come from the camp at Kosielsk?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Do you mean the camp at Kosielsk? Yes?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: The witness did not say that.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know that place and locality?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Do you mean Kosielsk? I do, yes. In 1940, in the month of August-at the end of August-I spent my leave there with my wife.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know whether there were Polish officers at that place in a Russian prisoner-of-war camp?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes, I know that.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Until what time did these prisoners of war remain there?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I do not know that for sure but at the end of August 1940 they were there. I am quite sure about that.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Do you know whether this camp, together with its inmates, fell into German hands?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Personally, that is, from my own observation, I do not know it; but according to rumors, it appears to have been the case. That is, of course, not my own testimony; I myself did not see it, but I heard about it only.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you hear what happened to these prisoners?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes, I heard, of course, that they remained there and could not be evacuated.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you hear what became of them?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I have already testified in my answers to the prosecutor that they were shot on the order of the German Command.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And where did these shootings take place?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Mr. Defense Counsel, you have apparently not heard my answers. I already testified that Mayor Menschagin said that they were shot in the neighborhood of Smolensk, but where he did not tell me.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: How many prisoners were involved?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Do you mean to say, how many were mentioned in the conversation with Menschagin? I do not understand your question. Do you mean to say according to the reports of Menschagin?

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What was the figure given to you by Menschagin?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Menschagin did not tell me any number. I repeat that this conversation took place on the last days of September 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you give us the name of an eyewitness who was present at this shooting or anyone who saw this shooting?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I believe that these executions were carried out under such circumstances that I think it scarcely possible that any Russian witnesses could be present.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Witness, you should answer the question directly. You were asked, "Can you give the names of anybody who was there?" You can answer that "yes" or "no" and then you can add any explanations necessary.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I will follow your instructions, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Can you give the name of anybody who saw the executions?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: No, I cannot name any eyewitness.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What German unit is supposed to have carried out the shootings?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I cannot answer that exactly. It is logical to assume that it was the construction battalion which was stationed there; but of course I could not know the exact organization of the German troops.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did the Poles involved here come from the camp at Kosielsk?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: In general, this was not mentioned in the conversations of that time, but I certainly do not know that; besides these might have been any other Polish prisoners of war who had not been at Kosielsk previously.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Did you yourself see Polish officers?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: I did not see them myself, but my students saw them, and they told me that they had seen them in 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: And where did they see them?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: On the road where they were doing repair work at the beginning of summer, 1941.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: In what general area or location?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: In the district of the Moscow-Minsk highway, somewhat to the west of Smolensk.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Can you testify whether the Russian Army Command had a report to the effect that Polish prisoners at the camp at Kosielsk had fallen into the hands of the Germans?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: No, I have no knowledge of that.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What is the name of the German official or employee with whom you talked at the Kommandantur?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Not in the Kommandantur, but in Menschagin's office. His name was Hirschfeld.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: What was his position?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: He was Sonderfuehrer of the 7th Detachment of the German Kommandantur in the town of Smolensk.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I have no further questions, Mr. President just another question or two, Mr. President. [Turning to the witness.] Were you punished by the Russian Government on account of your collaboration with the German authorities?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: No, I was not.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Are you at liberty?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Not only am I at liberty; but, as I have already stated, I am still professor at two universities.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Therefore, you are back in of lice.

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Smirnov, do you wish to re-examine?

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELL0R SMIRNOV
turns.text: No, Mr. President, I have no further questions to put to the witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Witness, do you know whether the man, whose name I understand to be Menschagin, was told about these matters or whether he himself had any direct knowledge of them?

turns.speaker: BAZILEVSKY
turns.text: From Menschagin's own words, I understood quite definitely that he had heard those things himself at the Kommandantur, particularly from Von Schwetz, who was the commander from the beginning of the occupation.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The witness can retire.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Mr. President, I beg the Tribunal to allow me to call as witness Marko Antonov Markov, a Bulgarian citizen, professor at the University of Sofia. [The interpreter Valev and the witness Markov took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Are you the interpreter?

turns.speaker: LUDOMIR VALEV (Interpreter)
turns.text: Yes, Sir.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you give us your full name?

turns.speaker: VALEV
turns.text: Ludomir Valev.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear before God and the Law-that I will interpret truthfully and to the best of my skill-the evidence to be given by the witness. [The interpreter repeated the oath.] flit; PRESIDENT: [To the witness.] Will you give us your full name, please?

turns.speaker: DR. MARKO ANTONOV MARKOV (Witness)
turns.text: Dr. Marko Antonov Markov.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear- as a witness in this case-that I will speak only the truth-being aware of my responsibility before God and the Law-and that I will withhold and add nothing. [The witness repeated the oath.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You may sit down.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Mr. President, this witness is examined, I would like to call to the attention of the Tribunal the fact that Dr. Stahmer asked the preceding witness a question which I understood went: How did it happen that the interpreters had the questions and the answers to your questions if you didn't have them before you? Now that question implied that Dr. Stahmer had some information that the interpreters did have the answers to the questions, and I sent a note up to the interpreters, and I have the answer from the lieutenant in charge that no one there had any answers or questions, and I think it should be made clear on the record.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, I think so, too.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: I was advised of this fact outside the courtroom. If it is not a fact, I wish to withdraw my statement. I was informed outside the courtroom from a trustworthy source. I do not recall the name of the person who told me, I shall have to ascertain it.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Such statements ought not be made by counsel until they have verified them.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: May I begin the examination of this witness, Mr. President?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The examination, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Witness, I beg you to tell us briefly, without taking up the time of the Tribunal with too many details, under what conditions you were included in the so-called International Medical Commission set up by the Germans in the month of April 1943 for the examination of the graves of Polish officers in the Katyn woods. I beg you, when answering me, to pause between the question I put to you and your own answer.

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: This occurred at the end of April 1943. While working in the Medico-Legal Institute, where I am still working, I was called to the telephone by Dr. Guerow.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The witness must stop before the interpreter begins. Otherwise, the voices come over the microphone together. So the interpreter must wait until the witness has finished his answer before he repeats it. Now, the witness has said-at least this is what I heard-that in April 1943 he was called on the telephone.

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: I was called to the telephone by Dr. Guerow, the secretary of Dr. Filoff who was then Prime Minister of Bulgaria. I was told that I was to take part) as representative of the Bulgarian Government, in the work of an international medical commission which had to examine the corpses of Polish officers discovered in the Katyn wood. Not wishing to go, I answered that I had to replace the director of my Institute who was away in the country. Dr. Guerow told me that according to an instruction of the Minister of Foreign Affairs, who had sent the telegram, it was precisely in order to replace him that I would have to go there. Guerow told me to come to the Ministry. There I asked him if I could refuse to comply with this order. He answered that we were in a state of war and that the Government could send anybody wherever and whenever they deemed it necessary. Guerow took me to the first secretary of the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Schuchmanov. Schuchmanov repeated this order and told me that we were to examine the corpses of thousands of Polish officers. I answered that to examine thousands of corpses would take several months, but Schuchmanov said that the Germans had already exhumed a great number of these corpses and that I would have to go, together with other members of the commission, in order to see what had already been done and in order to sign, as Bulgarian representative, the report of the proceedings which had already been drafted. After that, I was taken to the German Legation, to Counsellor Mormann, who arranged all the technical details of the trip. This was on Saturday; and on Monday morning, 26 April, I flew to Berlin. There I was met by an official of the Bulgarian Legation and I was lodged at the Hotel Adlon.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please answer the next question: Who took part in this so-called International Commission, and when did they leave for Katyn?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: On the next day, 27 April, we stayed in Berlin and the other members of the commission arrived there too.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Who were they?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: They were the following, besides myself: Dr. Birkle, chief doctor of the Ministry of Justice and first assistant of the Institute of Forensic Medicine and Criminology at Bucharest; Dr. Miloslavich, professor of forensic medicine and criminology at Zagreb University, who was representative for Croatia; Professor Palmieri, who was professor for forensic medicine and criminology at Naples; Dr. Orsos, professor of forensic medicine and criminology at Budapest; Dr. Subik, professor of pathological anatomy at the University of Bratislava and chief of the State Department for Health for Slovakia; Dr. Hajek, professor for forensic medicine and criminology at Prague, who represented the so-called Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia; Professor Naville, professor of forensic medicine at the University of Geneva, representative for Switzerland; Dr. Speleers, professor for ophthalmology at Ghent University, who represented Belgium; Dr. De Burlett, professor of anatomy at the University of Groningen, representing Holland; Dr. Tramsen, vice chancellor of the Institute for forensic medicine at Copenhagen University, representing Denmark; Dr. Saxen, who was professor for pathological anatomy at Helsinki University, Finland. During the investigations of the commission, a Dr. Costeduat was missing; he declared that he could attend only as a personal representative of President Laval. Professor Piga from Madrid also arrived, an elderly gentleman who did not take any part in the work of the commission. It was stated later that he was ill as a result of the long journey.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Were all these persons flown to Katyn?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: All these persons arrived at Katyn with the exception of Professor Piga.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Who besides the members of the commission left for Katyn with you?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: On the 28th we took off from Tempelhof Airdrome, Berlin, for Katyn. We took off in two airplanes which carried about 15 to 20 persons each.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Maybe you can tell us briefly who was there?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: Together with us was Director Dietz, who met us and accompanied us. He represented the Ministry of Public Health. There were also press representatives, and two representatives of the Ministry for Foreign Affairs.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I beg you to stop with these details and to tell me when the commission arrived in Katyn?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: The commission arrived in Smolensk on 28 April, in the evening.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: How many work days did the commission stay in Smolensk? I stress work days.

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: We stayed in Smolensk 2 days only, 29 and 30 April 1943, and on 1 May, in the morning, we left Smolensk.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: How many times did the members of the commission personally visit the mass graves in the Katyn wood?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: We were twice in the Katyn wood, that is, in the forenoon of 29 and 30 April.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I mean, how many hours did you spend each time at the mass graves?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: I consider not more than 3 or 4 hours each time.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Were the members of the commission present at least once during the opening of one of the graves?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: No new graves were opened in our presence. We were shown only several graves which had already been opened before we arrived.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Therefore, you were shown already opened graves, near which the corpses were already laid out, is that right?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: Quite right. Near these 'opened graves were exhumed corpses already laid out there.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Were the necessary conditions for an objective and comprehensive scientific examination of the corpses given to the members of the commission?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: The only part of our activity which could be characterized as a scientific, medico-legal examination were the autopsies carried out by certain members of the commission who were themselves medico-legal experts; but there were only seven or eight of us who could lay claim to that qualification, and as far as I recall only eight corpses were opened. Each of us operated on one corpse, except Professor Hajek, who dissected two corpses. Our further activity during these 2 days consisted of a hasty inspection under the guidance of Germans. It was like a tourists' walk during which we saw the open graves; and we were shown a peasant's house, a few kilometers distant from the Katyn wood, where in showcases papers and objects of various sorts were kept. We were told that these papers and objects had been found in the clothes of the corpses which had been exhumed.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Were you actually present when these papers were taken from the corpses or were they shown to you when they were already under glass in display cabinets?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: The documents which we saw in the glass cases had already been removed from the bodies before we arrived.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Were you allowed to investigate these documents, to examine these documents, for instance, to see whether the papers were impregnated with any acids which had developed by the decay of the corpses, or to carry out any other kind of scientific examination?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: We did not carry out any scientific examination of these papers. As I have already told you, these papers were exhibited in glass cases and we did not even touch them.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: But I would like you nevertheless to answer me briefly with "yes" or "no," a question which I have already put to you. Were the members of the commission given facilities for an objective examination?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: In my opinion these working conditions can in no way be qualified as adequate for a complete and objective scientific examination. The only thing which bore the character of the scientific nature was the autopsy which I carried out.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: But did I rightly understand you, that from the 11,000 corpses which were discovered only 8 were dissected by members of the commission.

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: Quite right.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Please answer the next question. In what condition were these corpses? I would like you to describe the state in which they were and also the state of the inner organs, the tissues, et cetera.

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: As to the condition of the corpses in the Katyn graves, I can only judge according to the state of the corpse which I myself dissected. The condition of this corpse was, as far as I could ascertain, the same as that of all the other corpses. The skin was still well preserved, was in part leathery, of a brown-red color and on some parts there were blue markings from the clothes. The nails and hair, mostly, had already fallen out. In the head of the corpse I dissected there was a small hole, a bullet wound in the back of the head. Only pulpy substance remained of the brain. The muscles were still so well preserved that one could even see the fibers of the sinews of heart muscles and valves. The inner organs were also mainly in a good state of preservation. But of course they were dried up, displaced, and of a dark color. The stomach showed traces of some sort of contents. A part of the fat had turned into wax. We were impressed by the fact that even when pulled with brute force, no limbs had detached themselves. I dictated a report, on the spot, on the result of my investigation. A similar report was dictated by the other members of the commission who examined corpses. This report was published by the Germans, under Number 827, in the book which they published.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: I would like you to answer the following question. Did the medico-legal investigations testify to the fact that the corpses had been in the graves already for 3 years?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: As to that question I could judge only from the corpse on which I myself had held a post mortem. The condition of this corpse, as I have already stated, was typical of the average condition of the Katyn corpses. These corpses were far removed from the stage of disintegration of the soft parts, since the fat was only beginning to turn into wax. In my opinion these corpses were buried for a shorter period of time than 3 years. I considered that the corpse which I dissected had been buried for not more than 1 year or 18 months.

turns.speaker: MR. COUNSELLOR SMIRNOV
turns.text: Therefore, applying the criteria of the facts which you ascertained to your experiences in Bulgaria- that is, in a country of a more southern climate than Smolensk and where decay, therefore, is more rapid-one must come to the conclusion that the corpses that were exhumed in the Katyn forest had been lying under the earth for not more than a year and a half? Did I understand you correctly?

turns.speaker: MARKOV
turns.text: Yes, quite right. I had the impression that they had been buried for not more than a year and a half.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal will adjourn now. [The Tribunal adjourned until 2 July 1946 at 1000 hours.]
